1	# Key Squares and the Rule of the Square
2	
3	> Key squares are the squares a king must reach to force a pawn's promotion; the rule of the square is a quick test for whether a lone king can catch a passed pawn.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–1900
6	**Prerequisites:** [Opposition](opposition.md), [King and Pawn vs King](king-and-pawn-vs-king.md)
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	In king-and-pawn endings the **pawn does not win by itself** — the king wins. For most pawns there is a small set of squares such that if the stronger side's king occupies one of them, promotion is guaranteed regardless of whose move it is. These are the **key squares**.
11	
12	For a non-rook pawn that has not yet crossed the middle of the board, the three key squares sit **two ranks ahead of the pawn**, on the pawn's file and the two adjacent files. Once the pawn reaches the 5th rank or beyond, the key squares are the three squares **directly in front of and beside the pawn one rank up** — and reaching any of them wins.
13	
14	The **rule of the square** answers a different, faster question: can a bare king stop a runner without help from its own king? Draw an imaginary square whose side equals the distance from the pawn to its promotion square. **If the defending king is inside that square (or can step into it on its move), it catches the pawn.** If it's outside and it's the pawn's move, the pawn queens.
15	
16	## Why it matters
17	
18	These two tools convert vague "I think this is winning" feelings into exact knowledge. The rule of the square lets you judge a pawn race in one second instead of counting tempo by tempo — invaluable in time trouble. Key squares tell you *where to aim your king* before pushing the pawn, so you don't let the defender grab the opposition and hold a draw. Almost every rook, minor-piece, and queen ending eventually simplifies into a king-and-pawn position; knowing the result there is what makes your earlier decisions correct.
19	
20	## Diagrams
21	
22	```
23	8 . . . . . . . .
24	7 . . . . . . . .
25	6 . . . . . . . .
26	5 . . . k . . . .
27	4 . . . P . . . .
28	3 . . . K . . . .
29	2 . . . . . . . .
30	1 . . . . . . . .
31	  a b c d e f g h
32	```
33	*White's key squares for the d4-pawn are c6, d6 and e6; reaching any of them wins.*
34	**FEN:** 8/8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/8 b - - 0 1
35	Side to move: Black.
36	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/8_b_-_-_0_1
37	
38	Here White holds the **opposition** (kings face off on the d-file with Black to move), and that is exactly what lets the king fight to a key square: **1...Kd6 2.Kc4 Kc6 3.d5+!** (the pawn push is a priceless spare tempo that clears d4 for the king) **3...Kd6 4.Kd4 Kd7 5.Ke5 Ke7 6.d6+ Kd7 7.Kd5 Kd8 8.Ke6** and the king reaches the key square e6, escorting the pawn home. Crucially, the *same* position with **White** to move is only a draw — whoever must move first loses the opposition. The key squares tell you the target; the opposition tells you whether you can get there.
39	
40	```
41	8 . . . . . . . .
42	7 . . . . . . . .
43	6 . . . . . . . .
44	5 . . . . . . . .
45	4 . . . . . k . .
46	3 . . . . . . P .
47	2 . . . . . . . .
48	1 . K . . . . . .
49	  a b c d e f g h
50	```
51	*The square of the g3-pawn spans c3–c8–g8–g3; the black king on f4 is inside it.*
52	**FEN:** 8/8/8/8/5k2/6P1/8/1K6 b - - 0 1
53	Side to move: Black.
54	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/8/8/5k2/6P1/8/1K6_b_-_-_0_1
55	
56	The pawn needs five moves to reach g8, so its square has side 5: corners c3, c8, g8, g3. The black king on f4 is **inside** that square, so it catches the runner at once: **1...Kg4** attacks the pawn, and after **2.g... ** it cannot escape — e.g. **2.Kc2 Kxg3**. If the king were on h2 instead — outside the square — and it were White to move, **1.g4** would run home untouched.
57	
58	## Patterns and sub-types
59	
60	- **Pawn on ranks 2–4 (not rook pawn):** key squares are two ranks ahead, three files wide.
61	- **Pawn on ranks 5–6:** the three squares one rank ahead (front and both diagonals) are key — reaching any wins outright.
62	- **Rook pawns are special:** they have **no winning key squares** for the attacker if the defending king reaches the corner; a/h pawns frequently draw.
63	- **Rule of the square shortcut:** look at the file the pawn stands on; the square's far corner is the promotion square and the same number of files across.
64	- **Pawn on its starting rank** can move two squares, so count from the **third** rank, not the second, when applying the rule.
65	- **Distant passed pawn / decoy:** use the rule to verify a pawn deflects the enemy king far enough for your own king to win elsewhere.
66	
67	## How to spot it
68	
69	```mermaid
70	flowchart TD
71	  A[King and pawn ending] --> B{Is the enemy king nearby}
72	  B -->|No it is far| C[Apply rule of the square]
73	  C -->|King inside square| D[Pawn is caught]
74	  C -->|King outside and pawn to move| E[Push and queen]
75	  B -->|Yes both kings present| F{Has my king reached a key square}
76	  F -->|Yes| G[Win with opposition and outflank]
77	  F -->|No| H[March king to key square first then push]
78	```
79	
80	## Worked examples
81	
82	```
83	8 . . . . . . . .
84	7 . . . . . . . k
85	6 . . . . . . . .
86	5 . . . . . . . .
87	4 . . . . . . . p
88	3 . . . . . . . .
89	2 . . . . . . . .
90	1 K . . . . . . .
91	  a b c d e f g h
92	```
93	*Can White's king catch the h-pawn? Count the square.*
94	**FEN:** 8/7k/8/8/7p/8/8/K7 w - - 0 1
95	Side to move: White.
96	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/7k/8/8/7p/8/8/K7_w_-_-_0_1
97	
98	The pawn is on h4; its square is h4–h1–e1–e4. White's king on a1 is **outside** the square. With White to move it cannot enter in time: **1.Kb2 h3 2.Kc2 h2 3.Kd2 h1=Q**. The pawn queens — the king on a1 was simply too far. (The far-off black king on h7 plays no part; this is a pure king-versus-pawn race.)
99	
100	```
101	8 . . . . . . . .
102	7 . . . . . . . .
103	6 . . . k . . . .
104	5 . . . P . . . .
105	4 . . . K . . . .
106	3 . . . . . . . .
107	2 . . . . . . . .
108	1 . . . . . . . .
109	  a b c d e f g h
110	```
111	*White has the opposition; Black to move must give ground and White reaches a key square.*
112	**FEN:** 8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/8/8 b - - 0 1
113	Side to move: Black.
114	https://lichess.org/analysis/8/8/3k4/3P4/3K4/8/8/8_b_-_-_0_1
115	
116	Pawn on d5; the key squares are c6, d6, e6. Because White holds the opposition, Black must step aside and White invades a key square: **1...Kd7 2.Kc5 Kc7 3.Ke6! Kd8 4.Kd6** (key square, opposition) **Kc8 5.Ke7 Kc7 6.d6+ Kc6 7.d7 Kc7 8.d8=Q+** and White wins. Note the king reaches a key square *before* the pawn advances — pushing too early lets Black blockade or stalemate. The very same position with **White** to move (Black holding the opposition) is only a draw.
117	
118	## Common mistakes
119	
120	- Pushing the pawn the moment you can, surrendering the opposition and the win.
121	- Counting the rule-of-the-square from the 2nd rank when the pawn can still jump two squares.
122	- Forgetting that **rook pawns** usually do not have winning key squares for the attacker.
123	- Including your own king's location when applying the rule of the square — the rule is about the *defending* king and pawn only.
124	- Miscounting whose move it is in a race; one tempo flips the result.
125	
126	## Train it
127	
128	- Drill the basic K+P vs K win from both sides: set up the first diagram and win it 10 times, alternating colors.
129	- Practice the rule of the square cold: deal yourself 20 random "lone king vs passer" positions and call caught/queens in under three seconds each.
130	- Lichess puzzle themes: **endgame** and **pawnEndgame**; filter for king-and-pawn.
131	- Books: Silman's *Complete Endgame Course* (first chapters), Dvoretsky's *Endgame Manual* (Pawn Endings — the precise key-square tables), and de la Villa's *100 Endgames You Must Know* (Endgames 1–10).
132	
133	## Exercises
134	
135	1. **FEN:** 8/8/8/8/3P4/8/8/k6K w - - 0 1 — White to move. Does White queen, or does the black king catch the pawn?
136	2. **FEN:** 8/8/8/4k3/4P3/4K3/8/8 b - - 0 1 — Black to move (so White has the opposition). Show how White reaches a key square and wins.
137	3. **FEN:** 8/8/8/8/8/5k2/4p3/4K3 b - - 0 1 — Black to move. Can Black promote, or does White hold?
138	4. **FEN:** 7k/8/8/8/P7/8/8/7K w - - 0 1 — White to move. Apply the rule of the square: result?
139	
140	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
141	
142	1. Pawn on d4; the king is queenside, so test the square d4–a4–a8–d8. The black king on a1 is **outside** it (rank 1 is below rank 4). With White to move **1.d5 Kb2 2.d6 Kc3 3.d7 Kc4 4.d8=Q** queens. White wins.
143	2. White already has the opposition; the key squares for the e4-pawn are d6, e6, f6. Black must give way and White outflanks to a key square: 1...Kd5 2.Kf4 Kd6 3.Kf5 Kd7 4.Kf6! (key square f6) Kd6 5.e5+ Kd7 6.Kf7 and the pawn runs e5–e6–e7–e8=Q. Symmetric on the queenside after 1...Kf5 2.Kd4. White wins.
144	3. **White holds the draw.** Black king f3, pawn e2, White king e1 — the defending king blockades the queening square, the classic drawing setup. With Black to move he cannot break in: 1...Ke3 2.Kf1! and the White king shuffles e1–f1, always returning in front of the pawn; if Black ever pushes e2–e1 the White king is right there to capture, and any king move that leaves e2 undefended runs into Kxe2. Black has no way to queen — **draw**.
145	4. Pawn on a4, square a4–a8–e8–e4. Black king on h8 is **outside** the square, and it is White to move: **1.a5 ... a8=Q**. White queens; the king cannot catch it.
146	
147	</details>
148

But note: a-pawn is a ROOK PAWN. The rule of the square still applies for catching (the corner draw is about the defender's king reaching the corner, but here black king h8 can't even catch it). 
With White to move 1.a5: black king h8 -> can it enter square? king h8 to catch a8-pawn: needs to reach a8 area. h8->g7... it's way outside. White 1.a5 Kg7 2.a6 Kf6 3.a7 ... 4.a8=Q. Black king nowhere near. CORRECT. White queens. SOUND.
